summary: DHH reflects on two bloggers he admires, Mark Pilgrim and Michael Barrish, who share deeply personal stories online with courageous honesty. He argues that their vulnerability isn't purposeless—it functions as social currency, earning respect and admiration from strangers worldwide. He acknowledges his own inability to overcome the pride and fear that prevent such openness.
key_points: Radical honesty in public writing earns genuine respect and connection from strangers
Vulnerability online is not a void trade but an exchange of social currency
Pride and fear are the barriers that hold most people back from honest self-disclosure
money_quotes: Their honesty isn't being offered as a void trade. It's social currency that buys the respect and aspiration of strangers all around the world.
I wish I too could overcome the barrier of pride and fear holding back honesty.
key_insight: Public vulnerability is not weakness but a form of social currency that earns genuine respect and human connection from strangers.
